questions.prompt: Project Alpha: invest $1, get $2 back (NPV = $0.82, IRR = 100%). Project Beta: invest $1,000,000, get $1,200,000 back (NPV ≈ $90,900, IRR = 20%). Cost of capital = 10%. Mutually exclusive — which do you pick?
choices: Beta — higher NPV means more dollar value created
questions.explanation: NPV correctly compares dollar value created and is the right criterion for mutually exclusive choices. IRR rewards percentage returns and can grossly misrank by scale.

questions.prompt: A mining project has cash flows -100, +230, -132 (cleanup at end). How many real IRRs does this have?
questions.explanation: Two sign changes → up to two IRRs. This project has IRR = 10% and IRR = 20% (both make NPV = 0). The IRR rule is ambiguous here; NPV is not.

questions.prompt: Which of the following are legitimate uses of the payback period rule?
choices: Quick liquidity screen for early-stage startups with limited cash runway
Primary decision criterion for selecting between mutually exclusive 30-year projects
Definitive ranking when NPV and IRR disagree
questions.explanation: Payback is useful as a quick screen and for communication, never as the primary decision rule. It ignores TVM and tail cash flows.

questions.prompt: When do NPV and IRR always agree on accept/reject?
choices: For a single conventional project (one negative CF then all positive)
Only when the discount rate equals the IRR
questions.explanation: For conventional CFs and a single accept/reject decision (no ranking), NPV > 0 ⟺ IRR > cost of capital. Disagreements arise with non-conventional CFs or mutually exclusive choices.
